summary: I was part of Company E, 116th Infantry Regiment, which was under the 29th Infantry Division. This was during World War II, and I remember being inducted into the Army in 1943. My time with Company E was part of my early military experience after basic training.

The 29th Infantry Division was the larger unit that included my Company E, 116th Infantry Regiment. I was inducted into the Army in 1943 and assigned to this division. It was a significant part of my military service during the war.

I was born and lived in Minneapolis, Minnesota until my junior year of high school. My father was an attorney there with an office overlooking Nicollet Park. Minneapolis was where I developed my love for baseball, watching players like Ted Williams from my father's office window.

Nicollet Park in Minneapolis was right outside my father's office window where he practiced law. From there, I could see the baseball field and got to know the left fielders, including Ted Williams. This view probably fueled my obsession with baseball during my youth.

Ted Williams was one of the left fielders I got to know from watching baseball games at Nicollet Park in Minneapolis. Seeing him play up close from my father's office window left a lasting impression on me and contributed to my passion for baseball.

In the summer of 1942, my family moved to Milwaukee, Wisconsin, where I attended Shorewood High School. Milwaukee was also where I registered for the draft and was inducted into the Army at the Buffalo Street Induction Center. It was a pivotal place for me as I transitioned from high school to military service.

I went to Shorewood High School in Milwaukee after moving there in 1942. It was a good school, and I was on the Wisconsin baseball team. I graduated in June 1943, just before being inducted into the Army.

I was inducted into the Army at the Buffalo Street Induction Center in Milwaukee on June 7, 1943. The center was located in a warehouse district south of Wisconsin Avenue. It was where I took my physical and swore the oath to enter military service.

After being inducted in Milwaukee, I was sent by train to Rockford, Illinois, where Camp Grant was located. The trip included a transfer at Beloit, and I was with a group that included inmates from the Green Bay Reformatory. Rockford was the next step in my military processing.

Camp Grant near Rockford was where I underwent medical training and further processing after induction. I spent two or three weeks there, taking the Army General Classification Test and basic training before being sent to other assignments. It was a key part of my early Army experience.

During my induction process, I was placed temporarily with inmates from the Green Bay Reformatory. At that time, most inmates were white, unlike today. This experience was unusual and memorable as I was processed alongside these men before entering the Army.

Beloit was the train station where we changed trains en route from Milwaukee to Rockford. I remember sitting in the smoking room with some of the Green Bay inmates who bought whiskey during the stop. It was a brief but notable part of my journey to Camp Grant.

Before my induction, the military offered V-5 and V-7 Navy pilot training courses at my high school. These programs aimed to keep some men in college during the war. Although I passed the tests, I was nearsighted and thus ineligible for the Navy programs.

The V-7 program was another Navy pilot training course offered at my high school during my senior year. It was part of the military's effort to maintain educated men during the war. I passed the qualification tests but could not join due to my eyesight.

The Adjutant General of the Army sent me a letter confirming I had passed the qualification test for the Navy pilot programs. I carried that letter with me during my induction. It was a symbol of my potential to attend college and serve in a specialized capacity.

After Camp Grant, I was sent to Fort Hamilton in Brooklyn, New York, where I joined the 113th Infantry Regiment. The unit was mostly Italian-American, and I was the only one without an Italian-sounding name. Fort Hamilton was where I learned to handle weapons and experienced early Army life.

I was assigned to the 113th Infantry Regiment at Fort Hamilton, Brooklyn. The regiment was composed mainly of New York and New Jersey National Guard soldiers, mostly Italian-Americans. I made friends there and learned the basics of infantry duty.
cues: New York and New Jersey National Guard

summary: While stationed at Fort Hamilton, we sometimes patrolled the beaches on Long Island with a BAR and a dog. It was my first experience handling a gun and being on guard duty. Long Island was part of my early Army training environment.

There was talk of a strike among the soldiers at Fort Hamilton, and some were threatened with being sent to the Eastern Coastal Penal Colony at Camp Upton, New York. I was included in that group initially but was unaware of the details. This incident led to my transfer out of that unit.

Camp Upton in New York was where the Eastern Coastal Penal Colony was located. After the strike incident at Fort Hamilton, some soldiers, including me, were at risk of being sent there. Instead, I was transferred to a college training program in Florida.

I was sent to John B. Stetson University in Deland, Florida, as part of a college training program after leaving Fort Hamilton. Unfortunately, the program was shut down shortly after I arrived. While there, I attended classes and found ways to sneak out and enjoy campus life.

Deland, Florida, was where John B. Stetson University is located, and where I was sent for a college training program during the war. It was a change from military camps, and I got to experience campus life, even though the program was soon canceled. Deland was a brief but memorable stop in my Army journey.

After Stetson University, I was sent to Camp Blanding, Florida, an Infantry Replacement Training Center near St. Augustine and Jacksonville. The camp was on what used to be Claude Pepper's farm, and we trained intensively for 17 weeks. Camp Blanding was where I completed my basic infantry training.

St. Augustine, Florida, was near Camp Blanding where I trained as an infantry replacement. The area was part of my military environment during basic training. It was close to Jacksonville and Deland, places I also passed through during my service.

Jacksonville, Florida, was near Camp Blanding where I underwent infantry replacement training. It was part of the region where I spent much of my military training after leaving the college program. Jacksonville was a familiar city during my Army service in Florida.

Camp Blanding was located on land that had once been Claude Pepper's farm. Claude Pepper later became a Congressman or Senator, and his name was associated with the area. We even trained on terrain called Pepper Hill, linking the camp to his legacy.

Camp Blanding served as an Infantry Replacement Training Center where I completed 17 weeks of basic infantry training. The center was massive, with many training battalions cycling through. It was a rigorous and essential part of preparing soldiers for combat.

At Camp Blanding, I was assigned to the 190th Infantry Training Battalion, specifically C Company. Our squads were larger than usual, with 12 men each, and we trained constantly. This battalion was where I learned the skills needed for infantry combat.

Ronald Reagan was not mentioned in my military experiences or training, so I have no direct connection or memories involving him during my service. My story focuses on my induction, training, and early Army assignments during World War II.
